projects.question_en: Does an MRI quality-control pipeline (MRIQC) calibrated on healthy controls generalize to pre- and post-surgical trigeminal neuralgia patients, or do surgical/pathological changes cause systematic domain shift in QC metrics (CJV, CNR, SNR, EFC, FBER, FWHM, tSNR)?
projects.question_es: ¿Un pipeline de control de calidad de RM (MRIQC) calibrado en controles sanos generaliza a pacientes con neuralgia del trigémino pre- y posquirúrgicos, o los cambios quirúrgicos/patológicos provocan un desplazamiento de dominio sistemático en las métricas de QC (CJV, CNR, SNR, EFC, FBER, FWHM, tSNR)?
projects.dataset_name: A large-scale dataset of pre- and postsurgical MRI data from patients with chronic trigeminal neuralgia (OpenNeuro ds005713)

projects.question_en: Does a behavioral classifier that separates OSA patients from matched controls using the cognitive battery (PVT, SART, PPT, MST) trained on one session generalize to the other three sessions, or is 'cognitive impairment in OSA' only detectable at specific circadian/post-sleep timepoints?
projects.question_es: ¿Un clasificador conductual que separa pacientes con AOS de controles emparejados usando la batería cognitiva (PVT, SART, PPT, MST), entrenado en una sesión, generaliza a las otras tres, o el 'deterioro cognitivo en AOS' solo es detectable en momentos circadianos/post-sueño concretos?
projects.still_open_because: No paper trains a multivariate classifier separating OSA patients from matched controls on the PVT/SART/PPT/MST battery, and none tests train-on-one-session / test-on-other-sessions generalization; nothing found cites or reuses OpenNeuro ds008108. Existing work is adjacent and univariate: Djonlagic et al. (10.5664/jcsm.3692) and the CPAP follow-up (10.5664/jcsm.5794), plus 10.1371/journal.pone.0085918 and 10.5664/jcsm.8210, show OSA motor-sequence-learning deficits are timepoint-specific (overnight consolidation impaired, encoding largely spared), and 10.1007/s00702-015-1501-6 reviews group-level PVT/attention deficits. Deep-learning work on sustained attention in OSA (10.1007/s41782-021-00190-0) uses brain MRI, not the behavioral battery, with no cross-session analysis. Remaining delta: (a) subject-level discriminative classification from behavioral features rather than group-mean comparisons, and (b) an explicit session-transfer matrix quantifying whether the OSA signature is circadian/post-sleep-locked or session-invariant.
projects.dataset_name: A Comprehensive Multimodal Dataset for Investigating Cognitive Impairment in Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OpenNeuro ds008108)

projects.question_en: Does the ratio of peritumoural oedema volume to enhancing tumour volume on preoperative multiparametric MRI predict shorter overall survival in glioblastoma, independently of total tumour volume and age?
projects.question_es: ¿La razón entre el volumen de edema peritumoral y el volumen de tumor realzante en la RM multiparamétrica preoperatoria predice menor supervivencia global en glioblastoma, de forma independiente del volumen tumoral total y la edad?
projects.clinical_rationale: Extensive oedema is often read on rounds as a marker of infiltrative disease that limits how far a surgeon can safely resect, but whether it carries prognostic weight beyond raw tumour size changes how much it should influence the operative plan.
projects.outcome_measure: Overall survival (days; median OS and Cox hazard ratio per unit oedema:enhancement ratio)
projects.still_open_because: No identified study fits a multivariable Cox OS model that includes the oedema-to-enhancing-tumour ratio while adjusting for total tumour volume and age together; prior tests either regressed the ratio on OS univariately (null) or adjusted only for age (PFS-positive, OS-null). A confirmatory multivariable OS analysis with both covariates remains a thin but genuine gap.

projects.question_en: In the BraTS meningioma and brain-metastasis cohorts, which MRI descriptors (dural contact, degree of peritumoural oedema relative to lesion volume, lesion multiplicity, necrotic core fraction) best distinguish a solitary metastasis from a glioblastoma or an atypical meningioma before surgery?
projects.question_es: En las cohortes de meningioma y metástasis cerebral de BraTS, ¿qué descriptores de RM (contacto dural, grado de edema peritumoral relativo al volumen de la lesión, multiplicidad, fracción de núcleo necrótico) distinguen mejor una metástasis solitaria de un glioblastoma o un meningioma atípico antes de la cirugía?
projects.clinical_rationale: A solitary metastasis, a glioblastoma and an atypical meningioma each send the patient down a different path — systemic workup for a primary, en-bloc versus piecemeal resection, or Simpson-grade planning — and the call is often made on the preoperative MRI alone.
projects.outcome_measure: Preoperative diagnostic classification accuracy against the reference tumour-type label (AUC, confusion matrix, sensitivity for metastasis)
projects.still_open_because: No published work jointly ranks dural contact, edema-to-lesion-volume ratio, lesion multiplicity, and necrotic core fraction as interpretable discriminators across both the BraTS meningioma and brain-metastasis cohorts in a single three-class analysis that treats atypical meningioma as a confounder alongside glioblastoma; existing studies are pairwise and radiomics/deep-learning based rather than descriptor-level and interpretable.

projects.question_en: Is the structure of color-similarity experience stable across adult age and sex? Using the 21-59 year age range and near-balanced sex split, how much do behavioral dissimilarity geometry, ND-100 hue discrimination, and data quality (framewise displacement, tSNR) vary with age and sex — and, given n=31 usable participants, what effect sizes could this dataset actually detect?
projects.question_es: ¿Es estable la estructura de la experiencia de similitud de color a lo largo de la edad adulta y entre sexos? Usando el rango de 21 a 59 años y la división casi equilibrada por sexo, ¿cuánto varían la geometría conductual de disimilitud, la discriminación de matiz (ND-100) y la calidad de los datos (desplazamiento por fotograma, tSNR) con la edad y el sexo, y qué tamaños de efecto puede realmente detectar este dataset con n=31 participantes utilizables?
projects.still_open_because: Both halves of the question exist in adjacent form, but not on this dataset and not jointly. (1) Age-dependence of color-similarity structure is already published by the same group: Kawakita et al., "Comparing color qualia structures through a similarity task in young children versus adults" (10.1073/pnas.2415346122), plus the explicit exchange "Do color similarity judgments vary with age, and do they reveal anything about qualia?" (10.1073/pnas.2521506122) and the reply (10.1073/pnas.2525916122) — however these contrast children vs adults, not within-adult variation across 21-59 y. (2) Sex differences in ND-100 hue discrimination are published in an independent 356-student cohort (10.3389/fpsyg.2025.1722974), but with no link to similarity geometry or neuroimaging. (3) Individual-level alignment of color qualia structures among adults exists (10.64898/2026.02.13.705699) but does not model age/sex as predictors. (4) The ds005684 descriptor (10.1038/s41597-025-04511-0) reports FD/tSNR QC but performs no demographic moderation. Remaining delta: nobody has tested within-adult age/sex moderation of behavioral dissimilarity geometry AND ND-100 scores AND fMRI QC metrics in this specific cohort, nor reported the sensitivity/minimum-detectable-effect analysis for n=31 — which is likely to be the main finding (an underpowered null / precision statement), a modest but genuinely unfilled contribution.
projects.dataset_name: Color Qualia Similarity Judgment fMRI Dataset (OpenNeuro ds005684)

projects.question_en: Does a simple bedside prognostic model for severe TBI built on MIMIC-IV (age, GCS motor score, first-day vitals and labs) retain its calibration and discrimination when applied across the ~200 hospitals of eICU, and which hospital characteristics predict where the model over- or under-estimates mortality?
projects.question_es: ¿Un modelo pronóstico sencillo de cabecera para TCE grave construido en MIMIC-IV (edad, componente motor del GCS, constantes y laboratorios del primer día) mantiene su calibración y discriminación al aplicarse a los ~200 hospitales de eICU, y qué características del hospital predicen dónde el modelo sobre- o subestima la mortalidad?
projects.clinical_rationale: Prognostic scores are quoted to families as if they were universal, but a model that is well calibrated in a Boston academic ICU and badly miscalibrated in a small community ICU would systematically mislead exactly the units with least neurotrauma experience.
projects.outcome_measure: In-hospital mortality, reported as discrimination (AUC) and calibration (slope/intercept) per hospital
projects.still_open_because: No prior work decomposes eICU's ~200 hospitals to characterize per-site calibration drift of a bedside TBI mortality model or identifies which hospital characteristics (size, teaching status, region, case volume, unit type) predict systematic over- vs under-estimation of mortality; existing MIMIC-to-eICU TBI validation uses eICU as a single pooled cohort.

projects.question_en: In patients admitted to the ICU with spontaneous intracerebral or subarachnoid haemorrhage, does prophylactic anti-seizure medication (levetiracetam or phenytoin started without a documented seizure) associate with discharge disposition and in-hospital mortality, and how much does the prophylaxis rate vary between hospitals in eICU?
projects.question_es: En pacientes ingresados en UCI por hemorragia intracerebral o subaracnoidea espontánea, ¿la medicación anticrisis profiláctica (levetiracetam o fenitoína iniciada sin crisis documentada) se asocia al destino al alta y a la mortalidad intrahospitalaria, y cuánto varía la tasa de profilaxis entre hospitales en eICU?
projects.clinical_rationale: AHA guidelines recommend against routine seizure prophylaxis after ICH/SAH yet prescribing persists widely, and quantifying whether it helps or merely adds sedation and worse disposition would settle a recurrent rounds debate.
projects.outcome_measure: Discharge disposition (functional-outcome proxy) and in-hospital mortality; secondary: documented in-hospital seizure and hospital-level variation in prophylaxis rate
projects.still_open_because: The remaining delta is the eICU-specific, multi-hospital practice-variation component: no located work quantifies between-hospital variation in prophylactic ASM rates (e.g., median odds ratio / ICC across eICU sites) for spontaneous ICH/SAH, nor links that variation to discharge disposition and in-hospital mortality within eICU. The association direction (levetiracetam neutral, phenytoin harmful) is already established elsewhere, so novelty is confined to the database-specific descriptive practice-variation and casemix-adjusted disposition/mortality estimates in eICU.

projects.question_en: In severe TBI patients receiving osmotherapy in the ICU, does first-line hypertonic saline versus mannitol associate with different in-hospital mortality, ICU length of stay and rate of subsequent surgical decompression, after adjustment for baseline GCS, sodium, creatinine and injury severity?
projects.question_es: En pacientes con TCE grave que reciben osmoterapia en UCI, ¿la elección de suero salino hipertónico frente a manitol como primera línea se asocia a distinta mortalidad intrahospitalaria, estancia en UCI y tasa de descompresión quirúrgica posterior, tras ajustar por GCS basal, sodio, creatinina y gravedad de la lesión?
projects.clinical_rationale: Every neuro-ICU has a house preference between hypertonic saline and mannitol despite no definitive trial, and knowing which agent precedes fewer crash craniectomies or renal injuries would settle a daily bedside argument.
projects.outcome_measure: In-hospital mortality; secondary: ICU length of stay and rate of subsequent decompressive craniectomy
projects.still_open_because: No published adult severe-TBI cohort compares first-line hypertonic saline vs mannitol on the specific triad of in-hospital mortality, ICU length of stay, AND rate of subsequent surgical decompression, adjusted for baseline GCS, sodium, creatinine and injury severity. Existing adjusted clinical-outcome comparisons are pediatric; adult adult work is either an unreported RCT or limited to ICP/CPP physiologic endpoints. The decompressive-craniectomy outcome in an adjusted adult osmotherapy cohort is the principal remaining delta.

projects.question_en: In patients aged 65 and over admitted with traumatic intracranial haemorrhage, does pre-injury anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy predict a worse hospital course — measured as need for craniotomy, blood-product transfusion, and in-hospital mortality — and does the association differ between direct oral anticoagulants and warfarin?
projects.question_es: En pacientes de 65 años o más ingresados por hemorragia intracraneal traumática, ¿el tratamiento anticoagulante o antiagregante previo a la lesión predice un peor curso hospitalario —medido como necesidad de craneotomía, transfusión de hemoderivados y mortalidad intrahospitalaria— y difiere la asociación entre anticoagulantes orales directos y warfarina?
projects.clinical_rationale: The anticoagulated elderly fall patient is the single most common neurotrauma consult, and whether a DOAC really carries the same haematoma-expansion risk as warfarin determines how aggressively we reverse and how long we observe before discharge.
projects.outcome_measure: In-hospital mortality; secondary: craniotomy/craniectomy rate and transfusion requirement
projects.still_open_because: No prior study evaluates all three hospital-course endpoints together (need for craniotomy, blood-product transfusion, in-hospital mortality) in a 65-and-over traumatic intracranial haemorrhage cohort while also stratifying the antithrombotic effect by DOAC versus warfarin; existing works cover mortality alone, hemorrhage incidence alone, or DOAC-vs-warfarin on bleeding occurrence rather than this surgical/transfusion/mortality triad.

projects.question_en: Does the trajectory of the Glasgow Coma Scale over the first 72 ICU hours — its slope and variability — predict in-hospital mortality and discharge destination better than the single admission GCS in patients with traumatic brain injury?
projects.question_es: ¿La trayectoria de la escala de coma de Glasgow durante las primeras 72 h en UCI —su pendiente y variabilidad— predice mejor la mortalidad intrahospitalaria y el destino al alta que el GCS único de ingreso en pacientes con traumatismo craneoencefálico?
projects.clinical_rationale: Prognostic conversations with families still hinge on the admission GCS even though clinicians instinctively trust the trend more, and quantifying how much the 72-hour trajectory adds would change when withdrawal-of-care discussions are appropriate.
projects.outcome_measure: In-hospital mortality and discharge disposition; model comparison by AUC/net reclassification versus admission GCS alone
projects.still_open_because: The specific package is unpublished: (1) GCS trajectory characterized by both slope and variability (not just delta or improvement/deterioration), (2) the fixed first-72-ICU-hour window, (3) an explicit head-to-head incremental-value test versus single admission GCS, and (4) the dual outcome of in-hospital mortality AND discharge destination. Prior work covers pieces (serial/delta GCS prognosis; trajectory modeling of non-GCS variables) but not this combination.

projects.question_en: Can a simple bedside status epilepticus severity score built from ICU admission variables (age, level of consciousness, worst seizure type, prior seizure history — a STESS-like model) discriminate in-hospital mortality in MIMIC-IV status epilepticus patients, and does it outperform admission illness-severity alone?
projects.question_es: ¿Puede una escala sencilla de gravedad del estado epiléptico construida con variables de ingreso en UCI (edad, nivel de conciencia, peor tipo de crisis, antecedente de crisis previas —un modelo tipo STESS) discriminar la mortalidad intrahospitalaria en pacientes con estado epiléptico de MIMIC-IV, y supera a la gravedad de enfermedad al ingreso por sí sola?
projects.clinical_rationale: Families and intensivists want an early honest prognosis in status epilepticus, and validating whether the STESS score's simple components hold in a large ICU cohort tells us whether to trust it for goals-of-care conversations.
projects.outcome_measure: In-hospital mortality (discrimination by AUC); secondary: discharge disposition; benchmarked against an admission illness-severity baseline
projects.still_open_because: No published study specifically (a) isolates the MIMIC-IV status epilepticus subpopulation, (b) reconstructs/refits a STESS-like score from MIMIC-IV ICU admission variables, and (c) benchmarks its in-hospital mortality discrimination directly against admission illness-severity scores (SOFA / SAPS-II / APACHE) — i.e., an incremental-value comparison in this exact database and endpoint.

projects.question_en: In de novo Parkinson's disease patients followed in PPMI, how fast does the MDS-UPDRS Part III motor score (in the OFF-medication state) worsen over the first 3-5 years, and can baseline clinical variables (age at onset, tremor-dominant vs postural-instability/gait-difficulty phenotype, Hoehn & Yahr stage) identify the fast progressors who would reach the disabling-but-still-levodopa-responsive window where subthalamic DBS is usually considered?
projects.question_es: En pacientes con enfermedad de Parkinson de novo seguidos en PPMI, ¿con qué rapidez empeora la puntuación motora MDS-UPDRS Parte III (en estado OFF, sin medicación) durante los primeros 3-5 años, y pueden variables clínicas basales (edad de inicio, fenotipo tremor-dominante vs inestabilidad postural/trastorno de la marcha, estadio de Hoehn & Yahr) identificar a los progresadores rápidos que alcanzarían la ventana de discapacidad aún respondedora a levodopa en la que se suele considerar la DBS subtalámica?
projects.clinical_rationale: DBS timing is a recurring movement-disorder-clinic argument: refer too early and you operate on a mildly affected patient, too late and axial/cognitive decline erodes the benefit, so knowing which baseline phenotype predicts a fast motor slope directly informs when to send a patient to the functional neurosurgeon.
projects.outcome_measure: Annual rate of change (slope) in MDS-UPDRS Part III OFF-state score; secondary: time to Hoehn & Yahr stage 3
projects.still_open_because: No study has operationalized the 'disabling-but-still-levodopa-responsive STN-DBS candidacy window' as an explicit longitudinal endpoint in PPMI and tested whether baseline age at onset, TD/PIGD phenotype, and H&Y predict which de novo patients reach it within 3-5 years; existing work predicts generic fast progression, not this DBS-specific clinical milestone (which PPMI de novo patients rarely attain that early, so it must be surrogate-defined).

projects.question_en: Among genetically characterised Parkinson's disease patients in PPMI, do GBA carriers, LRRK2 carriers and non-carriers differ in the joint trajectory of motor (MDS-UPDRS III) and cognitive (MoCA) decline over follow-up, and is the GBA group's faster cognitive slope large enough to change the DBS candidacy conversation, given that cognitive impairment is a relative contraindication?
projects.question_es: Entre los pacientes con enfermedad de Parkinson genéticamente caracterizados en PPMI, ¿difieren los portadores de GBA, los portadores de LRRK2 y los no portadores en la trayectoria conjunta de deterioro motor (MDS-UPDRS III) y cognitivo (MoCA) durante el seguimiento, y es la pendiente cognitiva más rápida del grupo GBA lo bastante grande como para cambiar la conversación sobre candidatura a DBS, dado que el deterioro cognitivo es una contraindicación relativa?
projects.clinical_rationale: GBA-associated Parkinson's is widely believed to progress toward dementia faster, and since post-DBS cognitive decline is the outcome families most fear, quantifying the genotype-specific cognitive slope tells the surgeon whether GBA status should temper enthusiasm for stimulation.
projects.outcome_measure: Genotype-stratified slopes of MDS-UPDRS Part III and MoCA over time (mixed-effects model interaction term)
projects.still_open_because: No single study fits a JOINT/bivariate latent trajectory of MDS-UPDRS III and MoCA simultaneously across all three genotype groups (GBA / LRRK2 / non-carrier) within PPMI and then formally tests whether the GBA cognitive slope crosses a pre-specified DBS-candidacy threshold. Existing work models motor and cognitive progression separately, and the GBA-worse-post-DBS-cognition evidence comes from separate surgical cohorts, not from a PPMI trajectory analysis translated into a candidacy decision rule. The remaining delta is the integrated bivariate model plus an explicit, quantified candidacy-threshold analysis.

projects.question_en: Using paired OFF- and ON-medication MDS-UPDRS Part III assessments in PPMI, how much does baseline levodopa responsiveness (percentage improvement OFF to ON) vary between patients, and does it stay stable over the first few years of disease — the assumption that underlies its use as the single best preoperative predictor of subthalamic DBS motor benefit?
projects.question_es: Usando evaluaciones MDS-UPDRS Parte III pareadas en estado OFF y ON en PPMI, ¿cuánto varía entre pacientes la respuesta basal a levodopa (porcentaje de mejoría de OFF a ON) y se mantiene estable durante los primeros años de enfermedad —el supuesto que sustenta su uso como el mejor predictor preoperatorio del beneficio motor de la DBS subtalámica?
projects.clinical_rationale: The preoperative levodopa-challenge response is the number every functional neurosurgeon quotes when predicting how much a patient will improve after STN-DBS, yet its stability early in disease is rarely checked against a large cohort, and any drift would undermine referrals made years before surgery.
projects.outcome_measure: MDS-UPDRS Part III percent improvement OFF-to-ON at baseline and its longitudinal stability (within-patient variability over visits)
projects.still_open_because: No study quantifies the inter-patient variability of baseline levodopa responsiveness (percent OFF-to-ON improvement) in PPMI and tests whether that responsiveness is stable year-over-year across the first few years of disease, i.e., directly interrogating the temporal-stability premise that justifies using a single early levodopa-challenge response as the preoperative predictor of STN-DBS outcome. The predictor-validity work lives in surgical cohorts, not the de novo PPMI cohort, and the stability-over-time question is asserted rather than empirically measured.

projects.question_en: Using TCGA-LGG and TCGA-GBM collections, can simple preoperative MRI descriptors a radiologist can score by eye (tumour location and lobe, contrast enhancement pattern, presence of a non-enhancing tumour component, cortical involvement) classify IDH mutation and 1p/19q codeletion status well enough to guide the decision between upfront maximal resection and stereotactic biopsy?
projects.question_es: Usando las colecciones TCGA-LGG y TCGA-GBM, ¿pueden descriptores sencillos de RM preoperatoria evaluables visualmente por un radiólogo (localización y lóbulo, patrón de realce, presencia de componente no realzante, afectación cortical) clasificar el estado de mutación IDH y codeleción 1p/19q con precisión suficiente para orientar la decisión entre resección máxima inicial y biopsia estereotáctica?
projects.clinical_rationale: IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted gliomas benefit most from aggressive early resection, so a preoperative read that shifts the molecular prior changes whether the patient goes to craniotomy or to biopsy first.
projects.outcome_measure: Diagnostic accuracy for molecular class (AUC, sensitivity/specificity, and calibration against biopsy-confirmed IDH and 1p/19q status)

projects.question_en: How well does an automated seizure detector trained and validated on annotated scalp EEG (CHB-MIT and the TUH seizure subset) perform, and where do its sensitivity and false-alarm rate degrade — establishing the performance floor and pitfalls before such a tool could be trusted to flag non-convulsive seizures on ICU continuous EEG?
projects.question_es: ¿Qué rendimiento alcanza un detector automático de crisis entrenado y validado sobre EEG de cuero cabelludo anotado (CHB-MIT y el subconjunto de crisis de TUH), y dónde se degradan su sensibilidad y su tasa de falsas alarmas —estableciendo el suelo de rendimiento y sus limitaciones antes de que una herramienta así pueda usarse para detectar crisis no convulsivas en EEG continuo de UCI?
projects.clinical_rationale: Non-convulsive seizures in the ICU are missed without continuous EEG that few centres can review in real time, so knowing the achievable sensitivity and false-alarm burden of automated detection defines whether screening tools are anywhere near ready for the neuro-ICU.
projects.outcome_measure: Seizure-detection sensitivity, specificity and false alarms per hour, reported within-dataset and cross-dataset (CHB-MIT <-> TUH)
projects.still_open_because: No single study takes a detector trained on CHB-MIT+TUH (corpora that contain almost no non-convulsive seizures) and quantifies its sensitivity/FAR specifically against annotated ICU continuous-EEG non-convulsive seizures as the held-out domain-shift test set. That NCS-specific generalization number is the remaining delta, but it is a validation exercise on well-trodden benchmarks rather than a novel scientific question.

projects.question_en: Which PSG-derived sleep-architecture features (stage proportions, arousal-linked events, spectral power) predict the within-subject overnight change in vigilance (PVT) and memory (PPT/MST), and does the same feature set predict the change across the afternoon nap in the same person?
projects.question_es: ¿Qué características de arquitectura del sueño derivadas de la PSG (proporciones de estadios, eventos ligados a microdespertares, potencia espectral) predicen el cambio intrasujeto nocturno en vigilancia (PVT) y memoria (PPT/MST), y ese mismo conjunto predice el cambio a través de la siesta vespertina en la misma persona?
projects.still_open_because: Both halves are individually published, but not the combination. Mullins et al., SLEEP 2023 (10.1093/sleep/zsad087) already tests NREM slow-oscillation/delta power and slow/fast spindle density as correlates of OVERNIGHT declarative memory change (word-pair task pre/post PSG) in older adults with OSA vs controls; the Djonlagic line (10.1371/journal.pone.0034106; 10.1371/journal.pone.0085918; 10.1513/annalsats.202204-315oc) covers fragmentation-driven motor and emotional consolidation in sleep-disordered breathing. Separately, fragmentation/stage-shift metrics predict attentional slowing in OSA (10.3390/jpm16020117, 2026), but cross-sectionally, not as a within-subject overnight delta. Remaining delta: (1) no prior work applies ONE unified feature set (stage proportions + arousal-linked events + spectral power) jointly to BOTH vigilance (PVT) and memory (PPT/MST) overnight change in the same cohort, so shared-vs-dissociable predictor structure across cognitive domains is untested; (2) no prior work tests whether that same feature set predicts change across an AFTERNOON NAP in the SAME individuals. Existing nap-vs-night within-person work (10.1111/jsr.12968, Manoach group) establishes only feature-level correspondence (nap spindle density estimates nocturnal spindle density), not that the feature-to-cognitive-change mapping generalizes across sleep-episode type. That cross-episode generalization test is the novel contribution ds008108 enables.
projects.dataset_name: A Comprehensive Multimodal Dataset for Investigating Cognitive Impairment in Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OpenNeuro ds008108)

projects.question_en: Using the paired pre-surgical and ~6-month post-surgical MRI available for 58 patients, do longitudinal within-subject changes in structural MRI (e.g., FreeSurfer-derived subcortical/thalamic volumes from the T1w/T2w scans) track with surgical outcome (favorable vs. unfavorable pain reduction), pointing to a treatment-response correlate rather than a static pre-surgical biomarker?
projects.question_es: Usando la RM pareada pre- y ~6 meses posquirúrgica disponible para 58 pacientes, ¿los cambios longitudinales intrasujeto en la RM estructural (p. ej., volúmenes subcorticales/talámicos derivados con FreeSurfer a partir de las secuencias T1w/T2w) se relacionan con el desenlace quirúrgico (reducción de dolor favorable vs. desfavorable), señalando un correlato de respuesta al tratamiento en lugar de un biomarcador estático prequirúrgico?
projects.still_open_because: The core hypothesis — that longitudinal within-subject structural change tracks surgical pain relief in TN (treatment-response correlate, not static biomarker) — is already established by DeSouza et al. 2015 (Pain, 10.1097/j.pain.0000000000000156), which showed reversal of insular cortical and trigeminal-nerve microstructural abnormalities after effective surgery. Separately, the same ds005713 dataset already has an outcome paper (Chizhikov/Brain Communications 2025, 10.1093/braincomms/fcaf178) but it used ONLY pre-surgical T1w imaging as a static predictor and did not exploit the paired post-surgical scans. The specific remaining delta: nobody has yet run the pre→~6mo-post within-subject longitudinal analysis on ds005713's 58 paired patients using FreeSurfer subcortical/thalamic volumetry, contrasting favorable vs. unfavorable pain outcomes. That exact execution (this cohort, subcortical/thalamic volumes, graded outcome stratification of longitudinal change) is unpublished, though it is a modality/cohort variation on an already-demonstrated concept.
projects.dataset_name: A large-scale dataset of pre- and postsurgical MRI data from patients with chronic trigeminal neuralgia (OpenNeuro ds005713)

projects.question_en: Does a multivariate classifier trained to decode option-set size (3 vs 9 vs 24) from BOLD activity generalize to held-out subjects and held-out runs, or does it only work within-subject?
projects.question_es: ¿Un clasificador multivariado entrenado para decodificar el tamaño del conjunto de opciones (3 vs 9 vs 24) a partir de la señal BOLD generaliza a sujetos y a corridas no vistas, o solo funciona intra-sujeto?
projects.still_open_because: Reutskaja et al. 2018 (Nat Hum Behav, 10.1038/s41562-018-0440-2) is the canonical choice-overload fMRI study and establishes that BOLD in dorsal striatum and ACC varies with option-set size — but it is entirely univariate/parametric GLM at the group level (inverted-U of set-size-related signal), with no classifier, no single-trial pattern decoding, and no test of whether set size is decodable from spatial patterns. Adjacent multivariate work exists only in other domains: a generalizable electrophysiological (EEG) signature of working-memory load (10.1111/psyp.13691) and an EEG/HDDM study of choice set size in choice overload (10.1016/j.neuroimage.2025.121542) — both EEG, neither fMRI MVPA, and the EEG choice-overload paper does not test leave-one-subject-out transfer. Zhang et al. (10.1162/jocn_a_01207) report vlPFC value updating by choice-set size, again univariate. No located work trains an fMRI classifier to decode 3 vs 9 vs 24 options, and none compares within-subject/within-run cross-validation against leave-one-run-out and leave-one-subject-out generalization. The remaining delta — whether set-size information is carried in a subject-general spatial code or is idiosyncratic (and whether apparent decoding is driven by run-level drift or trial-count/RT confounds) — is genuinely unanswered. Note also that Reutskaja used set sizes 6/12/24, not 3/9/24, so the stated dataset may be a different collection; the novelty judgment holds either way.

projects.question_en: Do individual differences in cortical thickness and surface area of frontoparietal control regions, measured from the T1w scan, predict the steepness of each participant's BOLD response to increasing option-set size?
projects.question_es: ¿Las diferencias individuales en grosor cortical y área de superficie de regiones frontoparietales de control, medidas en la imagen T1w, predicen la pendiente de la respuesta BOLD de cada participante al aumento del número de opciones?
projects.still_open_because: The group-level half is published: Reutskaja et al. 2018 (Nat Hum Behav, 10.1038/s41562-018-0440-2) established that BOLD in dorsal striatum and ACC scales non-monotonically (inverted-U) with option-set size — so "BOLD response to increasing option-set size" is itself a known, characterized effect, and any new analysis must be framed as moderation of an already-described slope, not discovery of it. What no located work does is treat the per-participant slope as a phenotype and regress it on T1w-derived FreeSurfer morphometry (cortical thickness and surface area) of frontoparietal control regions. Structure-function individual-difference designs of exactly this shape exist in other domains (e.g. Cercor 2021 10.1093/cercor/bhaa322, independent contributions of dlPFC structure and function to working memory; Cercor 2026 10.1093/cercor/bhag089, parietal thickness predicting V5 motion responses), which shows the method is standard but has not been applied to set-size sensitivity. Remaining delta: (1) no published test of thickness/surface-area prediction of set-size BOLD slope in any dataset, and (2) no such analysis on the Choice Overload fMRI Dataset specifically. Two caveats to design around before committing a student: the effect is non-monotonic, so a single "steepness" scalar is under-specified (need to define slope over the ascending limb, or fit a quadratic and use the linear/quadratic coefficients separately); and typical single-site choice-overload samples (n roughly 20-40) are badly underpowered for brain-structure-to-brain-function individual-difference correlations, plus surface-based structure-function correlations carry a documented spurious-correlation risk from shared surface reconstruction and smoothing (10.1162/imag_a_00478) that must be explicitly controlled.

projects.question_en: Because decoy presence and option count were experimentally manipulated in a full 2x3 design, is the neural effect of adding a decoy causally modulated by set size (i.e., is there a decoy x set-size interaction), and where in the brain?
projects.question_es: Dado que la presencia de senuelo y el número de opciones se manipularon experimentalmente en un diseno factorial 2x3 completo, ¿el efecto neural de anadir un senuelo esta causalmente modulado por el tamano del conjunto (es decir, existe interaccion senuelo x tamano) y en que regiones?
projects.still_open_because: The dataset's own source paper — Stanley & Wedell, "Neural Dynamics of Multiattribute Decision Making Under Choice Overload," bioRxiv 10.1101/2025.11.18.689103 (2025-11-18, Univ. of South Carolina) — is exactly this 2 (decoy present/absent) x 3 (3/9/24 options) fMRI design, so the question is not novel in setup. BUT every neural analysis they report deliberately collapses the crossed factor: (a) set-size effects are "linear and quadratic contrasts ... tested in separate GLMs, averaging across the levels of the second experimental factor (decoy vs no decoy)", with contrast vectors literally written as [-1 2 -1 -1 2 -1] and [-1 0 1 -1 0 1] — i.e. decoy-collapsed by construction; (b) the decoy effect is a decoy-present > decoy-absent one-sample t-test "collapsing across set size". They report main effects only: DLPFC inverse-U and ACC linear with set size; DLPFC + anterior insula for decoy > no-decoy (notably NO ACC decoy effect, which they flag as a puzzle). They never estimate or report a Decoy x Number interaction contrast anywhere in the imaging analysis, whole-brain or ROI. Interaction terms appear only in the BEHAVIORAL data, where they ARE significant (Number x Decoy on satisfaction, F(1,51)=5.68, p=.02; on "right number of options", F(1,51)=4.49, p<.05; and the decoy effect itself shrinks with set size, F(1,51)=20.32, p<.001) — which makes the untested neural interaction a well-motivated, directly predicted gap rather than a fishing expedition. Remaining delta: fit the full 2x3 factorial second-level model on this dataset and test the decoy x set-size interaction (and simple decoy effects at 3 vs 9 vs 24) in ACC/DLPFC/AIns/striatum, which could also explain the paper's unresolved null ACC decoy effect if the ACC decoy response is set-size-dependent and cancels when collapsed. Note also the closest published near-neighbor Reutskaja et al. 2018 (Nat Hum Behav) manipulated set size only, without a crossed decoy factor.

projects.question_en: Where are the load-sensitive frontoparietal peaks in this task located in each individual, and how far do they shift from the group-average MNI coordinate — i.e., would a group-coordinate TMS target actually hit the functionally active tissue in each participant?
projects.question_es: ¿Dónde se ubican, en cada individuo, los picos frontoparietales sensibles a la carga de eleccion, y cuánto se desplazan respecto a la coordenada MNI promedio del grupo? Es decir, ¿un blanco de EMT basado en coordenadas grupales realmente alcanzaría el tejido funcionalmente activo de cada participante?
projects.still_open_because: The core scientific claim is already established, not open. Sack et al. 2009 (J Cogn Neurosci, 10.1162/jocn.2009.21126) directly compared individual fMRI-guided TMS targeting against group-average Talairach/MNI coordinates, 10-20 positioning, and probabilistic targeting for a frontoparietal cognitive task, and showed group coordinates substantially degrade functional hit rate and effect size (requiring ~5x the sample). Sparing et al. 2008 (HBM) and Lynch/Dosenbach precision-mapping work (10.1016/j.xpro.2023.102118; 10.1016/j.biopsych.2023.11.022) further document that individual functional peaks deviate on the order of 1-2 cm from group centroids and that individual-specific network targeting is required. The only remaining delta is descriptive and dataset-local: nobody has published the per-subject peak coordinates and group-to-individual Euclidean displacement distribution for load-sensitive frontoparietal activation specifically in the Choice Overload fMRI Dataset. That is a replication of a known effect in a new sample, so it can yield a dataset characterization but not a novel finding — the answer ("no, a group coordinate would frequently miss") is predictable from prior work. To be genuinely novel the project would need an added angle, e.g. linking individual peak displacement to a behavioral choice-overload parameter, or comparing task-peak vs resting-state-network-derived targets with an electric-field model in the same subjects.

projects.question_en: Does the neural representational geometry of the nine hues generalize across people and across the report/no-report context — i.e., can a color-similarity model fit on N-1 participants' ventral occipitotemporal patterns predict the held-out participant's behavioral dissimilarity matrix, and does a decoder trained on report trials still work on no-report trials?
projects.question_es: ¿La geometría representacional neural de los nueve matices generaliza entre personas y entre contextos de reporte/no-reporte? Es decir, ¿un modelo de similitud de color ajustado en N-1 participantes (patrones en corteza occipitotemporal ventral) predice la matriz de disimilitud conductual del participante excluido, y un decodificador entrenado en ensayos con reporte sigue funcionando en ensayos sin reporte?
projects.still_open_because: No paper performs the proposed neural analyses on ds005684. The data descriptor itself (Hirao et al., Sci Data 2025, 10.1038/s41597-025-04511-0) contains NO MVPA, NO RSA, and NO report/no-report decoder test — its Technical Validation is limited to behavioral split-half/inter-participant correlations, FD/tSNR, and a univariate one-sample t-test map. All 7 works citing it were checked: none re-analyze the fMRI data for color geometry. The adjacent prior work is (a) Kawakita et al., iScience 2025, "Is my 'red' your 'red'? Evaluating structural correspondences between color similarity judgments using unsupervised alignment" (10.1016/j.isci.2025.112029) plus Tsuchiya-lab follow-ups (10.1073/pnas.2415346122; preprint 10.64898/2026.02.13.705699) — these establish cross-individual generalization of color qualia geometry BEHAVIORALLY only, never from brain patterns; and (b) Nakagi et al., iScience 2025 (10.1016/j.isci.2025.112427), which aligns neural representational structures across individuals and brain areas but for natural scenes, not the nine hues, and never predicts a held-out participant's behavioral RDM. Remaining delta: (1) leave-one-participant-out neural (VOTC) -> held-out behavioral color RDM prediction has not been done for color in any dataset; (2) report -> no-report cross-condition decoder transfer for hue identity is untested — the closest no-report structural work (Kirchberger/Tsuchiya-adjacent, 10.1093/nc/niaf053) manipulates spatial attention behaviorally for shapes/faces, not hue decoding in fMRI. Caveat for the student: the behavioral-generalization half of the question is largely settled, so the contribution must be framed on the neural side, and N in ds005684 is small enough that leave-one-out RSA reliability should be power-checked first.
projects.dataset_name: Color Qualia Similarity Judgment fMRI Dataset (OpenNeuro ds005684)

projects.question_en: Do out-of-scanner perceptual and affective phenotypes predict in-scanner neural color coding — specifically, does a participant's ND-100 Hue discrimination score (and Ishihara score, STAI, BDI-II, sleepiness) explain between-subject variance in the fidelity of hue decoding and in behavior-brain RDM correspondence?
projects.question_es: ¿Los fenotipos perceptivos y afectivos medidos fuera del escáner predicen la codificación neural del color dentro del escáner? En concreto, ¿la puntuación del test ND-100 Hue (y el Ishihara, STAI, BDI-II y somnolencia) explica la varianza entre sujetos en la fidelidad de decodificación del matiz y en la correspondencia entre las matrices de disimilitud conductual y neural?
projects.still_open_because: No paper links out-of-scanner phenotypes to in-scanner neural color coding in this dataset. Prior work covers the two halves separately: (1) the data descriptor itself, Nakagawa/Tsuchiya et al., "A neuroimaging dataset during sequential color qualia similarity judgments with and without reports," Sci Data 2025 (10.1038/s41597-025-04511-0), releases ds005684 and reports that each participant's hue discriminability was measured outside the scanner, but performs only technical-validation analyses — no hue decoding, no behavior-brain RDM correspondence, and no individual-difference regression on the hue/questionnaire scores; a EuropePMC citation/full-text search returned the descriptor as the ONLY record mentioning ds005684, i.e. no downstream analysis paper exists yet. (2) Individual differences in color qualia structure have been characterized purely behaviorally by Kawakita/Tsuchiya et al., "Is my 'red' your 'red'?", iScience 2025 (10.1016/j.isci.2025.112029) and the 2026 preprint "Robust individual alignment of color qualia structures" (10.64898/2026.02.13.705699), which align per-subject behavioral color similarity structures (including color-atypical observers) but never touch fMRI. (3) Neural-side individual variability via unsupervised alignment exists for natural scenes, not color (10.1016/j.isci.2025.112427). Remaining delta: nobody has tested whether ND-100 Hue / Ishihara scores, or affective-state measures (STAI, BDI-II, sleepiness), explain between-subject variance in hue decoding fidelity or in behavior-brain RDM correspondence. Two caveats for the student: verify that STAI/BDI-II/sleepiness are actually in the ds005684 participants.tsv (the descriptor abstract mentions only the hue test), and note that N=35 with a restricted, mostly color-normal Ishihara range gives low power for individual-difference correlations — the effect would need to be large to detect.
projects.dataset_name: Color Qualia Similarity Judgment fMRI Dataset (OpenNeuro ds005684)

projects.question_en: Do representations from pretrained vision and vision-language foundation models predict human color-similarity brain activity better than classical colorimetric models? Benchmark model-derived RDMs for the nine calibrated hues (CIE/HSV distance, CNN and CLIP-style embeddings of the color patches) against the behavioral dissimilarity RDM and the ventral occipitotemporal neural RDM, in both report and no-report trials.
projects.question_es: ¿Las representaciones de modelos de fundacion de vision y vision-lenguaje predicen la actividad cerebral durante juicios de similitud de color mejor que los modelos colorimetricos clasicos? Comparar matrices de disimilitud derivadas de modelos para los nueve matices calibrados (distancia CIE/HSV, embeddings tipo CNN y CLIP de los parches de color) frente a la matriz conductual y a la matriz neural occipitotemporal ventral, en ensayos con y sin reporte.
projects.still_open_because: The BEHAVIORAL half is already well covered. Kawakita et al. 2024 (Sci Rep, 10.1038/s41598-024-65604-1) aligned human color-similarity structures (93 colors) against LLM-derived color similarity structures using Gromov-Wasserstein optimal transport, and Marjieh et al. 2024 (Sci Rep, 10.1038/s41598-024-72071-1) showed GPT-family models (including a vision-co-trained GPT-4) predict human pairwise color similarity, explicitly comparing text-only vs. visual-input models and recovering the color-wheel geometry against classical color-space structure. Related work also exists benchmarking DNN color coding (10.1371/journal.pone.0253442) and MEG color-space geometry (10.1016/j.cub.2020.10.062). What remains genuinely open: (1) no published work links foundation-model image embeddings (CNN / CLIP-style patch embeddings) to a NEURAL color RDM in human ventral occipitotemporal cortex — the prior model-vs-human comparisons are behavior-only; (2) ds005684 (Hirao et al. 2025, 10.1038/s41597-025-04511-0) has only its own data-descriptor technical validation and no reuse papers found in EuropePMC (query "ds005684" OR "color qualia similarity" returned only the descriptor, its preprint, and an unrelated methods paper); (3) the report vs. no-report contrast — i.e., whether model-RDM fit to vOT is driven by the reporting/decision process or survives without overt report — has no precedent in this literature. Note the nine-hue stimulus set is small (36 pairs), which limits power to separate CIE/HSV from CNN/CLIP RDMs, so the neural + no-report angle, not the behavioral benchmark, is where the novelty lies.
projects.dataset_name: Color Qualia Similarity Judgment fMRI Dataset (OpenNeuro ds005684)

projects.question_en: How much do ICUs vary in their early management of severe TBI — use of osmotherapy, deep sedation and vasopressors in the first 24 hours — and does that between-hospital variation explain differences in risk-adjusted mortality after accounting for APACHE score and admission GCS?
projects.question_es: ¿Cuánto varían las UCI en el manejo precoz del TCE grave —uso de osmoterapia, sedación profunda y vasopresores en las primeras 24 h— y explica esa variación entre hospitales las diferencias en mortalidad ajustada por riesgo tras tener en cuenta el APACHE y el GCS de ingreso?
projects.clinical_rationale: If risk-adjusted TBI mortality differs several-fold between ICUs that follow the same guidelines, the difference is a modifiable process-of-care problem rather than case mix, which is precisely the argument for regionalising neurotrauma care.
projects.outcome_measure: Risk-adjusted in-hospital mortality, modelled with hospital-level random effects (median odds ratio)
projects.still_open_because: Remaining delta: the specific variance-partition question — how much between-hospital variation in these three early (first-24h) treatments explains risk-adjusted in-hospital mortality once APACHE and admission GCS are accounted for. Prior CENTER-TBI work uses GCS/IMPACT adjustment and 6-month GOSE functional outcome and treats therapies as an aggregate intensity index, not APACHE-adjusted mortality decomposed by these three specific early therapies; the APACHE framing implies a generic critical-care registry (ANZICS/ICNARC/MIMIC-type) not yet applied to this question.

projects.question_en: Among ICU patients whose status epilepticus fails first-line benzodiazepines, does the choice of second-line anti-seizure drug (levetiracetam vs valproate vs fosphenytoin/phenytoin) associate with different rates of treatment failure — needing a third agent or a continuous anaesthetic infusion — and ICU length of stay across the ~200 hospitals of eICU?
projects.question_es: En pacientes de UCI cuyo estado epiléptico no responde a benzodiacepinas de primera línea, ¿la elección del fármaco anticrisis de segunda línea (levetiracetam vs valproato vs fosfenitoína/fenitoína) se asocia a distintas tasas de fracaso terapéutico —necesidad de un tercer agente o de infusión anestésica continua— y a distinta estancia en UCI, a lo largo de los ~200 hospitales de eICU?
projects.clinical_rationale: ESETT found the three second-line agents equivalent in a randomised trial, but real-world multicentre practice and outcomes still vary, and knowing whether one agent precedes fewer escalations informs the drug a resident reaches for at 2 a.m.
projects.outcome_measure: Treatment failure (escalation to third-line agent or continuous anaesthetic); secondary: ICU length of stay and in-hospital mortality
projects.still_open_because: No published analysis performs a real-world comparative-effectiveness study of second-line ASD choice in the eICU multi-hospital cohort using escalation-to-third-agent/anaesthetic-infusion and ICU LOS as outcomes; ESETT answers efficacy (seizure cessation) in an RCT but not these real-world resource/escalation endpoints or heterogeneity across ~200 ICUs.

projects.question_en: In severe-TBI patients across the ~200 eICU hospitals, how strongly does a single documented episode of hypotension (systolic BP < 90 mmHg) in the first 24 ICU hours predict in-hospital mortality after adjustment for admission GCS, APACHE score, age and early vasopressor use, and does the effect size hold consistently between hospitals?
projects.question_es: En pacientes con TCE grave a lo largo de los ~200 hospitales de eICU, ¿con qué fuerza un único episodio documentado de hipotensión (presión sistólica < 90 mmHg) en las primeras 24 h de UCI predice la mortalidad intrahospitalaria tras ajustar por GCS de ingreso, APACHE, edad y uso precoz de vasopresores, y se mantiene ese tamaño de efecto de forma consistente entre hospitales?
projects.clinical_rationale: The Brain Trauma Foundation guidelines treat even one prehospital or early hypotensive episode as a secondary insult that roughly doubles mortality, and quantifying it across many hospitals tells the bedside team how hard to defend a blood-pressure floor in the first day.
projects.outcome_measure: In-hospital mortality (adjusted odds ratio for any first-24h SBP < 90 episode; consistency assessed with hospital-level random effects)
projects.still_open_because: No located study formally tests whether the adjusted hypotension-to-mortality effect size is consistent across the ~200 eICU hospitals (a multilevel/random-effects cross-hospital heterogeneity analysis), nor uses the exact adjustment set (GCS + APACHE + age + early vasopressor use) on a binary single-episode SBP<90 exposure in eICU. The underlying predictive association itself, however, is already well established.

projects.question_en: In severe-TBI patients across the eICU hospitals, is in-ICU beta-blocker exposure (from the medication table) associated with lower in-hospital mortality after adjustment for admission GCS, APACHE score, age and first-day heart rate, and is any association concentrated in patients with early tachycardia suggestive of sympathetic hyperactivity?
projects.question_es: En pacientes con TCE grave a lo largo de los hospitales de eICU, ¿la exposición a betabloqueantes en la UCI (según la tabla de medicación) se asocia a menor mortalidad intrahospitalaria tras ajustar por GCS de ingreso, APACHE, edad y frecuencia cardíaca del primer día, y se concentra esa asociación en pacientes con taquicardia precoz sugestiva de hiperactividad simpática?
projects.clinical_rationale: Observational series (the DASH-after-TBI literature) repeatedly suggest beta-blockade blunts the catecholamine surge and improves survival after severe TBI, but with no definitive RCT a multi-hospital signal helps decide whether propranolol belongs in the early order set.
projects.outcome_measure: In-hospital mortality (adjusted odds ratio for beta-blocker exposure; effect modification tested in the early-tachycardia subgroup)
projects.still_open_because: No existing study performs this analysis in the eICU-CRD multicenter cohort specifically, AND — the genuinely open delta — none pre-specifies and tests the effect-modification hypothesis that any beta-blocker mortality benefit is concentrated in patients with early (first-day) tachycardia as a proxy for sympathetic hyperactivity. Prior work reports main/adjusted effects and generic subgroups but not a formal heart-rate-stratified interaction test tying benefit to the hyperadrenergic phenotype. Remaining delta = (1) eICU replication + (2) the tachycardia-by-beta-blocker interaction analysis.

projects.question_en: In the ENIGMA-Parkinson's distributed meta-analysis, how large and how consistent across sites are the differences in subcortical volumes and cortical thickness between Parkinson's patients and controls, and do the structural measures that differ most reproducibly overlap with the basal-ganglia and thalamic targets used in functional neurosurgery?
projects.question_es: En el metaanálisis distribuido ENIGMA-Parkinson, ¿qué magnitud tienen y cuán consistentes entre sitios son las diferencias en volúmenes subcorticales y grosor cortical entre pacientes con Parkinson y controles, y las medidas estructurales que difieren de forma más reproducible se solapan con las dianas de ganglios basales y tálamo empleadas en neurocirugía funcional?
projects.clinical_rationale: Before any structural measure can be trusted as a marker relevant to the surgical targets, it has to hold up across scanners and sites rather than at one center, and ENIGMA's multi-site meta-analytic design is exactly what shows whether a Parkinson's structural signature is robust enough to take seriously.
projects.outcome_measure: Cross-site effect size (Cohen's d) of subcortical volume and cortical-thickness differences (PD vs control) with between-site heterogeneity (I-squared)
projects.still_open_because: No published work explicitly cross-references ENIGMA-PD's most-reproducible (largest-d, lowest-heterogeneity) structural measures against basal-ganglia/thalamic neurosurgical targets (STN/GPi/VIM-thalamus). This overlap analysis is also constrained because the standard ENIGMA subcortical segmentation does not include STN, so any mapping is limited to thalamus, pallidum, and putamen.

projects.question_en: Within subjects, how does brain activation to the goal-priming task (match versus mismatch, promotion versus prevention conditions) change from before to after the six-week rTMS-plus-psychotherapy protocol, and how large is that change relative to the task's own test-retest noise?
projects.question_es: Dentro de cada sujeto, ¿cómo cambia la activación cerebral ante la tarea de priming de metas (coincidencia frente a no coincidencia, condiciones de promoción frente a prevención) desde antes hasta después del protocolo de seis semanas de rTMS más psicoterapia, y qué tamaño tiene ese cambio frente al propio ruido test-retest de la tarea?
projects.still_open_because: Davis, Beynel, Neacsiu, Luber, Bernhardt, Lisanby & Strauman (2023, Int J Clin Health Psychol, 10.1016/j.ijchp.2023.100382) analyze exactly this cohort and exactly this idiographic goal-priming task before and after the combined rTMS/psychotherapy protocol — so the pre-post design is not new. But that paper reports task-based *functional connectivity/connectome* change at the network level (DMN, cerebellum, limbic hyperconnectivity; fronto-parietal/visual hypoconnectivity, normalizing toward healthy-volunteer patterns). It does not report univariate BOLD activation for the task's own design contrasts (match vs mismatch crossed with promotion vs prevention), and it provides no reliability benchmark. Related prior work characterizes those activation contrasts only cross-sectionally in single sessions (Strauman et al. 2007, 10.1162/jocn.2007.19.7.1152; Eddington et al. 2009, 10.1093/scan/nsp016; Detloff et al. 2020, 10.1017/pen.2019.13). The remaining delta is (a) within-subject pre-post change in the match/mismatch x promotion/prevention activation contrasts themselves, and (b) scaling that change against the task's own test-retest noise (e.g., ICC / within-subject SDC from the repeated sessions, including the non-rTMS-active comparison arm as a change-free reference) — no published work estimates reliability for this task, so effect sizes for treatment-related activation change are currently uninterpretable.
projects.dataset_name: Individualized fMRI-guided rTMS combined with Self-System Therapy versus mindfulness exercises in treatment-resistant depression (OpenNeuro ds008064)

projects.question_en: Do published resting-state functional-connectivity signatures of major depression, derived on large non-refractory cohorts, still separate these 28 treatment-resistant patients from open normative control data, or do they fail out of domain?
projects.question_es: ¿Las firmas publicadas de conectividad funcional en reposo para la depresión mayor, derivadas de grandes cohortes no refractarias, siguen separando a estos 28 pacientes resistentes al tratamiento de datos normativos abiertos de control, o fallan fuera de dominio?
projects.still_open_because: The core claim — that rs-fMRI connectivity signatures of MDD degrade badly when moved out of their training domain — is already well established, so the headline finding would be a confirmation, not a discovery. Yamashita et al. 2020 (PLoS Biol, 10.1371/journal.pbio.3000966) built MDD network markers on large multi-site cohorts and explicitly tested transfer to fully independent cohorts and other disorders; Yamashita/Ichikawa 2020 (10.3389/fpsyt.2020.00400) quantified the multi-center generalization gap directly; Flint et al. 2021 (10.1038/s41386-021-01020-7) showed depression-classifier accuracies are systematically overestimated and collapse toward chance out of sample; Dinga et al. 2019 (10.1016/j.nicl.2019.101674, preprint 10.1101/416321) failed to replicate the Drysdale connectivity biotypes. The remaining delta is narrow and mostly a special case: none of these tested transfer specifically into a *treatment-resistant*, heavily medicated, rTMS-trial-enrolled sample (a clinical stage shift, not just a site shift), and none paired patients with controls pulled from a *separate* open dataset. That pairing is also the design's fatal weakness — with ds008064 patients (n=28) and controls from a different acquisition, scanner/site/protocol is perfectly confounded with diagnosis, so a "signature still separates groups" result is uninterpretable and a "fails out of domain" result is unattributable between clinical-stage effects and site effects. To be publishable the delta would need reframing (e.g. multiple independent control datasets to bound the site-effect term, or normative-modeling deviation scores rather than raw classification), and n=28 gives very little power to say anything precise about degradation magnitude.
projects.dataset_name: Individualized fMRI-guided rTMS combined with Self-System Therapy versus mindfulness exercises in treatment-resistant depression (OpenNeuro ds008064)

projects.question_en: Among ICU patients with severe traumatic brain injury and documented intracranial hypertension, does early decompressive craniectomy (within 48 h of admission) compared with continued maximal medical management change in-hospital survival and the odds of being discharged home rather than to a skilled nursing or long-term facility, when the comparison is set up as a target-trial emulation with propensity matching on admission GCS, age, pupillary/vital-sign status and injury severity?
projects.question_es: En pacientes de UCI con traumatismo craneoencefálico grave e hipertensión intracraneal documentada, ¿la craniectomía descompresiva precoz (en las primeras 48 h) frente al manejo médico máximo continuado modifica la supervivencia intrahospitalaria y la probabilidad de alta a domicilio en lugar de a un centro de cuidados prolongados, planteando la comparación como emulación de ensayo diana con emparejamiento por puntaje de propensión sobre GCS de ingreso, edad, estado pupilar/hemodinámico y gravedad de la lesión?
projects.clinical_rationale: RESCUEicp and DECRA left neurosurgeons with the uncomfortable result that craniectomy buys survival but often at the cost of severe disability, so real-world data on who actually walks out to home rather than to a nursing facility directly informs the consent conversation at 3 a.m.
projects.outcome_measure: In-hospital mortality and discharge disposition (home vs facility) as a proxy for dichotomised GOS-E; secondary: ICU length of stay
projects.still_open_because: The survival question is answered by RESCUEicp (RCT; 6-month GOS-E, not disposition) and related propensity studies, but no study emulates a target trial in an adult ICU severe-TBI + documented intracranial-hypertension cohort with the discharge-home-versus-facility disposition outcome and the specified early-timing (<48h) contrast; the disposition endpoint and the TTE-on-observational-data framing for this exact PICO remain unaddressed.

projects.question_en: In adults admitted to the ICU with status epilepticus (ICD-9/10 G40.x/G41.x), does receiving a guideline-adequate first-line benzodiazepine dose (e.g., lorazepam >=4 mg or midazolam >=10 mg within the first hour) versus an underdose reduce progression to refractory status epilepticus — defined as initiation of a continuous IV anaesthetic infusion (midazolam, propofol or pentobarbital) — and in-hospital mortality, after adjusting for admission severity and seizure aetiology?
projects.question_es: En adultos ingresados en UCI con estado epiléptico (CIE-9/10 G40.x/G41.x), ¿recibir una dosis inicial adecuada de benzodiacepina de primera línea (p. ej., lorazepam >=4 mg o midazolam >=10 mg en la primera hora) frente a una infradosis reduce la progresión a estado epiléptico refractario —definido como el inicio de una infusión anestésica intravenosa continua (midazolam, propofol o pentobarbital)— y la mortalidad intrahospitalaria, tras ajustar por gravedad al ingreso y etiología?
projects.clinical_rationale: Repeated audits show first-line benzodiazepines are chronically underdosed and that underdosing is the strongest modifiable predictor of a refractory course, yet clinicians hold back at the bedside for fear of respiratory depression.
projects.outcome_measure: Progression to refractory SE (initiation of continuous IV anaesthetic infusion); secondary: in-hospital mortality and intubation
projects.still_open_because: No existing study, in an administrative/ICD-9/10-coded ICU cohort, operationalizes refractory SE specifically as initiation of continuous IV anaesthetic infusion and uses it as a co-primary endpoint with in-hospital mortality while jointly adjusting for admission severity and seizure aetiology; prior work reports surrogate outcomes (ventilation days, ICU LOS, favorable discharge) from trial-embedded or single-city registry samples. This administrative-cohort emulation (feasible in MIMIC/eICU) is the remaining delta, though it is confirmatory of an already-established association rather than a novel hypothesis.

projects.question_en: Among ICU patients with severe traumatic brain injury, does placement of an invasive intracranial pressure monitor or external ventricular drain (identified from procedure codes and its timing relative to admission) change in-hospital mortality and the odds of discharge home rather than to a facility, when confounding by indication is handled with a target-trial emulation and propensity matching on admission GCS, age, pupillary status and injury severity?
projects.question_es: En pacientes de UCI con traumatismo craneoencefálico grave, ¿la colocación de un monitor invasivo de presión intracraneal o un drenaje ventricular externo (identificados por códigos de procedimiento y su momento respecto al ingreso) modifica la mortalidad intrahospitalaria y la probabilidad de alta a domicilio en lugar de a un centro, cuando la confusión por indicación se aborda con una emulación de ensayo diana y emparejamiento por propensión sobre GCS de ingreso, edad, estado pupilar y gravedad de la lesión?
projects.clinical_rationale: The BEST:TRIP trial failed to show a mortality benefit for ICP-monitor-guided management over clinical-and-imaging management, so real-world data on who actually benefits from an invasive monitor still shapes whether a bolt or EVD goes in at the bedside.
projects.outcome_measure: In-hospital mortality and discharge disposition (home vs facility) as a proxy for dichotomised GOS-E; secondary: ICU length of stay
projects.still_open_because: No study applies a formal target-trial emulation design (with explicit handling of immortal-time bias from monitor-placement timing) to this device question — existing work uses propensity weighting/matching in ordinary cohorts, and the only TBI target-trial emulation found addresses prophylactic anticoagulation, not ICP monitoring. Also not jointly done in one study: discharge-home-vs-facility as a co-primary outcome alongside mortality, and an explicit EVD-vs-ICP-bolt device-type contrast. That methodological+outcome combination is the remaining delta.

projects.question_en: How much does the analysable sample shrink and shift as inclusion criteria tighten in this release - from 108 participants with rest, to 54 with SCT, to 19 with complete ID event coverage - and do results from the complete-data subsample differ systematically from the maximal-sample results?
projects.question_es: ¿Cuanto se reduce y se desplaza la muestra analizable a medida que se endurecen los criterios de inclusion en esta version - de 108 participantes con reposo, a 54 con SCT, a 19 con cobertura completa de eventos en ID - y difieren sistematicamente los resultados de la submuestra con datos completos respecto a los de la muestra maxima?

projects.question_en: In TCGA-GBM and TCGA-LGG patients with survival follow-up, is the apparent survival advantage of larger resections explained by confounding with age, tumour location (eloquent vs non-eloquent) and molecular subtype, when analysed with propensity-score adjustment rather than simple group comparison?
projects.question_es: En pacientes de TCGA-GBM y TCGA-LGG con seguimiento de supervivencia, ¿la aparente ventaja de supervivencia de las resecciones más amplias se explica por confusión con edad, localización tumoral (elocuente vs no elocuente) y subtipo molecular, al analizarse con ajuste por puntaje de propensión en lugar de comparación simple de grupos?
projects.clinical_rationale: Surgeons select healthier patients with more accessible tumours for aggressive resection, so the survival benefit quoted in retrospective series may be partly selection rather than surgery — which matters when counselling a borderline candidate.
projects.outcome_measure: Overall survival (propensity-score-adjusted hazard ratio and adjusted median OS difference)
projects.still_open_because: The exact TCGA-GBM/TCGA-LGG propensity-score reanalysis, and specifically the inclusion of eloquent-vs-non-eloquent tumour location as a confounder (a variable TCGA does not natively provide), has not been published; prior work establishes the molecular-subtype confounding on institutional cohorts but not on TCGA and not with this three-way propensity-score design.

projects.question_en: Are there sleep-EEG signatures in the nocturnal PSG (slow-wave activity, spindle density and coupling, spectral slope) that track overnight memory consolidation and rs-fMRI network reorganization well enough to serve as candidate targets for closed-loop acoustic or transcranial stimulation in OSA?
projects.question_es: ¿Existen firmas de EEG de sueño en la PSG nocturna (actividad de ondas lentas, densidad y acoplamiento de husos, pendiente espectral) que sigan la consolidación de memoria nocturna y la reorganización de redes en rs-fMRI lo bastante bien como para ser dianas candidatas de estimulación acústica o transcraneal en lazo cerrado en la AOS?
projects.still_open_because: The EEG-half of the question is already answered. Djonlagic et al., "Overnight declarative memory consolidation and non-rapid eye movement sleep electroencephalographic oscillations in older adults with obstructive sleep apnea" (SLEEP 2023, 10.1093/sleep/zsad087) directly tested SWA, spindle density and SO-spindle coupling against overnight memory retention in OSA. Parekh et al., "EEG slow oscillations and overnight spatial navigational memory performance in CPAP-treated obstructive sleep apnea" (SLEEP 2025, 10.1093/sleep/zsaf046) repeats this for SOs; "Association between sleep microarchitecture and cognition in obstructive sleep apnea" (SLEEP 2024, 10.1093/sleep/zsae141), Zhang et al. (JCM 2023, 10.3390/jcm12020634) and the pediatric spindle work (10.1016/j.ejpn.2018.02.003) cover spindle-cognition links; region-specific hd-EEG + memory change after CPAP is covered by 10.1093/sleep/zsad255. Remaining delta, none of which any retrieved paper does: (1) no study links these PSG microstructural metrics to rs-fMRI network reorganization (DMN/FPN/salience) measured in the same OSA participants — existing OSA rs-fMRI work (e.g. 10.3389/fneur.2023.1215882, 10.1002/brb3.70698) is imaging-only with no concurrent sleep-EEG or overnight memory task; (2) the aperiodic/1-f spectral slope has not been evaluated as a consolidation-related marker in OSA (spectral-slope work in OSA is confined to sleep-depth staging, 10.1093/sleep/zsaf130); (3) no closed-loop acoustic or transcranial stimulation study exists in OSA at all — the closed-loop literature (10.1093/sleep/zsad206, 10.3390/brainsci8120204) is in healthy sleepers, so an explicit effect-size-based target-nomination analysis for OSA is unclaimed. Framing the project as a tri-modal (PSG microstructure -> overnight memory -> rs-fMRI network change) mediation plus stimulation-target feasibility analysis in ds008108 preserves novelty; framing it as "do SWA/spindles predict overnight memory in OSA" does not.
projects.dataset_name: A Comprehensive Multimodal Dataset for Investigating Cognitive Impairment in Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OpenNeuro ds008108)

projects.question_en: Does self-supervised pretraining on the 142 unlabeled full-night PSG recordings yield representations that beat a supervised baseline in the low-label regime (10-30 labeled subjects) on OSA-vs-control classification and on prediction of next-morning PVT vigilance decline?
projects.question_es: ¿El preentrenamiento autosupervisado sobre los 142 registros de PSG nocturna sin etiquetar produce representaciones que superen a una línea base supervisada en régimen de pocas etiquetas (10-30 sujetos) en la clasificación AOS-vs-control y en la predicción del descenso de vigilancia (PVT) a la mañana siguiente?
projects.still_open_because: The core methodological claim is already established, not open. Banville et al., "Uncovering the structure of clinical EEG signals with self-supervised learning" (J Neural Eng 2021, doi:10.1088/1741-2552/abca18) explicitly demonstrated that SSL pretext tasks on unlabeled sleep EEG beat fully-supervised baselines precisely in the low-labeled-subject regime, with performance gaps that close as labels increase — the exact experimental design proposed here. Subsequent work has generalized it: a multimodal sleep foundation model pretrained on large unlabeled PSG (Nat Med 2026, doi:10.1038/s41591-025-04133-4), label-efficient SSL decoding of wearable physiology (doi:10.1038/s44172-025-00467-6), and OSA-specific SSL (Self-Supervised Speech Representations for Sleep Apnea Severity Prediction, doi:10.1109/tbme.2026.3656326; AcceleRest masked autoencoder, doi:10.64898/2026.01.28.26345056). Two genuine deltas remain: (1) no published work uses ds008108 at all — zero hits for the accession or its dataset description; (2) prediction of next-morning PVT vigilance decline from PSG-derived SSL representations is unaddressed — searches for PSG + PVT + ML returned only protocols, cohort descriptions and conference abstracts, no predictive-modeling paper. Caveat for the student: 142 recordings is one to three orders of magnitude below the corpora used in the SSL-beats-supervised literature, so a null result would be uninformative about SSL and merely confirm the known scaling requirement; the defensible framing is the PVT-decline target (a novel label) and benchmarking against an off-the-shelf sleep foundation model rather than re-running the settled SSL-vs-supervised label-efficiency comparison.
projects.dataset_name: A Comprehensive Multimodal Dataset for Investigating Cognitive Impairment in Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OpenNeuro ds008108)

projects.question_en: In patients with trigeminal neuralgia, does combining structural connectivity (DTI-derived fractional anisotropy along trigeminal/pain-related white-matter tracts) with functional connectivity (resting-state fMRI) explain more variance in self-reported average pain intensity (BPI-facial) than either modality alone?
projects.question_es: En pacientes con neuralgia del trigémino, ¿combinar la conectividad estructural (anisotropía fraccional derivada de DTI en tractos de sustancia blanca relacionados con el trigémino/dolor) con la conectividad funcional (fMRI en reposo) explica más varianza en la intensidad de dolor promedio autoreportada (BPI-facial) que cualquiera de las dos modalidades por separado?
projects.still_open_because: Adjacent work exists on structural-functional relationships in trigeminal neuralgia — structure-function coupling analyses (10.1186/s10194-026-02419-7 on coupling plasticity; structural connectome work 10.3389/fnins.2026.1794457) and a systematic review of advanced TN neuroimaging (10.1097/j.pain.0000000000003365) — but none tests the specific question: whether COMBINING DTI-derived FA along trigeminal/pain tracts with resting-state functional connectivity explains INCREMENTAL variance in self-reported average pain intensity (BPI-facial) beyond either modality alone. Prior coupling studies characterize structure-function relationships or gene-expression correlates, not a head-to-head multimodal vs. single-modality predictive model of patient-reported pain severity, and none use the ds005713 cohort. The remaining delta is the explicit variance-partitioning / model-comparison of combined vs. unimodal connectivity predicting BPI-facial pain.
projects.dataset_name: A large-scale dataset of pre- and postsurgical MRI data from patients with chronic trigeminal neuralgia (OpenNeuro ds005713)

projects.question_en: Can embeddings from a pretrained 3D brain-MRI foundation model, applied to this dataset's defaced T1w/T2w structural images, few-shot classify trigeminal neuralgia patients vs. matched healthy controls more accurately (and with fewer labeled examples) than a convolutional network trained from scratch on this ~160-subject cohort?
projects.question_es: ¿Los embeddings de un modelo de fundación de RM cerebral 3D preentrenado, aplicados a las imágenes estructurales T1w/T2w desfiguradas (defaced) de este dataset, clasifican en modo few-shot a pacientes con neuralgia del trigémino frente a controles sanos emparejados con mayor precisión (y con menos ejemplos etiquetados) que una red convolucional entrenada desde cero en esta cohorte de ~160 sujetos?
projects.dataset_name: A large-scale dataset of pre- and postsurgical MRI data from patients with chronic trigeminal neuralgia (OpenNeuro ds005713)

projects.question_en: Does a volumetric prognostic threshold for enhancing tumour burden derived in one BraTS contributing institution still separate long- from short-survivors when applied unchanged to patients from the other institutions and scanners?
projects.question_es: ¿Un umbral volumétrico pronóstico de carga tumoral realzante derivado en una institución contribuyente de BraTS sigue separando supervivientes largos de cortos al aplicarse sin cambios a pacientes de las demás instituciones y escáneres?
projects.clinical_rationale: Cut-offs quoted in tumour board ('over 30 cc does badly') are usually derived at a single centre, and a surgeon needs to know whether the number survives a different scanner and referral pattern before letting it steer an operative decision.
projects.outcome_measure: Overall survival (discrimination of the threshold across sites: C-index, calibration slope, log-rank between strata within each held-out site)
projects.still_open_because: The remaining delta is the explicit cross-institution/cross-scanner transportability test of a FIXED (unrecalibrated) enhancing-tumor-burden threshold on BraTS multi-site data — i.e., does a cutoff derived at one contributing site retain its survival-stratifying power when applied verbatim to patients from other sites/scanners, quantifying site/scanner-induced threshold drift. Existing work reports prognostic association or thresholds within pooled or single cohorts, not the held-fixed transport across the BraTS site/scanner distribution.

projects.question_en: What is the causal effect of the act of reporting on the neural representation of color qualia? Because the circle/square cue randomly assigns each trial to report or no-report, the report minus no-report contrast is a within-subject randomized manipulation: which regions carry hue information in both conditions (candidate qualia correlates) versus only when a report is required (post-perceptual/reporting correlates), and does prefrontal activity statistically mediate the report-related change in ventral hue decoding?
projects.question_es: ¿Cuál es el efecto causal del acto de reportar sobre la representacion neural de las qualia de color? Dado que la clave (circulo/cuadrado) asigna cada ensayo a reporte o no-reporte, el contraste reporte menos no-reporte es una manipulación aleatorizada intrasujeto: ¿que regiones contienen informacion de matiz en ambas condiciones (candidatas a correlato de la quale) frente a solo cuando se exige un reporte (correlatos post-perceptivos/de reporte), y media la actividad prefrontal el cambio en la decodificacion ventral asociado al reporte?
